Case Study · 2023–2024

Designing clarityinto a multi-persona SaaS

B2B SaaS Workflow Design AI Exploration Systems Thinking

Part of a two-designer team at Phionike working on Brand Intelle — an enterprise SaaS used by marketing and finance teams. Over roughly eight months I personally owned the navigation redesign and the RFP module, while collaborating with the second designer on shared design direction, design system work, and AI exploration.

⚠

This case study covers the areas I personally led. Some specifics are adapted to respect NDA.

Role
UI/UX Designer
Company
Phionike
Timeline
~8 months · 2023–2024
Focus
Workflows · Navigation · RFP module

Overview

The project at a glance

Role
UI/UX Designer at Phionike
Team
Two-designer team · separate workstreams
Duration
~8 months · 2023–2024
My ownership
Navigation redesign · RFP module · interaction patterns
Skills used
UX Design Interaction Design Systems Thinking AI Exploration Design Systems Stakeholder Collaboration
What got shipped

By the end of my eight months on Brand Intelle, the redesigned navigation was live, the RFP module had shipped end-to-end, and a contextual drawer pattern I'd prototyped was being used to surface document context across procurement screens. A handful of early AI explorations were also part of the conversation — some moved forward, some were honestly better off descoped.

Problem

What I walked into

Brand Intelle sits across marketing and finance workflows, which means complexity layers on complexity — different roles, different goals, shared screens. As part of a two-designer team I focused on specific areas, but the underlying problems were ones we both lived with. Requirements often arrived verbally, clear in intent but undefined in flow.

Ambiguity Tight Timelines Continuous Iteration Technical Constraints
01
Multiple user roles, one product
Marketing and finance teams shared the same platform with different goals — and no UX distinction between their journeys.
02
Tightly coupled, data-heavy modules
Budgeting, vendors, and procurement were interconnected. A change in one often broke logic in another, which made design decisions expensive.
03
No established UX foundation
The product had grown quickly without a design system or shared interaction patterns. Friction was everywhere because nothing was decided once.
04
Pressure to add AI
Real pressure to introduce AI features, but no framework yet for where it would actually add value versus add noise.
Process

Collaborative, not linear

The work was iterative and rarely tidy. Both designers worked inside the same flow — syncing at the seams, owning separate workstreams in the middle. Feedback was continuous rather than stage-based, which meant scoping had to happen as we went.

01
Intake
Verbal briefs
02
Translate
Flows · Gaps
03
Map
In system
04
Iterate
PMs · CEO
05
Handoff
Dev specs
Scroll to zoom  ·  Drag to pan  ·  Double-click to reset
Early sessions usually started on paper — sketching flows before they hit Figma kept the conversation about logic, not pixels.
Stakeholders
Product Managers Developers CEO Business Team
Key Work

What I built

Two large areas of ownership — Navigation and the RFP module — plus one smaller interaction pattern I proposed and prototyped, the Contextual Right Drawer, which came directly out of friction I kept noticing in procurement flows.

Deliverable 01
Home & Navigation Experience
Orientation for a complex enterprise platform
Home and navigation redesign — structured to help users orient, prioritise, and move between interconnected modules.

The platform held a number of interconnected modules — Brief, RFP, NFA, approvals, execution — that all touched each other. The biggest problem wasn't any single screen; it was that users needed to move through the platform without losing their place. Home wasn't a destination, it was supposed to be a launchpad.

Core thought
Treat navigation not as a menu, but as a productivity layer — something that helps users understand the system, prioritise actions, and move with confidence.
Key focus areas
Reduce cognitive load
Users shouldn't have to think about where things live.
Always-clear hierarchy
No dead ends. You always know where you are and where to go next.
Surface priority work
Pending actions and live status on the Home screen — not a static landing page.
Cross-module flow
Contextual pathways reduce the cost of moving between modules.
Component detail
A closer view of a single nav entry point.
Honest note

An AI-driven home experience was explored early on, then descoped after feasibility reviews. In retrospect that was the right call — what we shipped was something the team could actually maintain.

Deliverable 02
RFP Module
Structured vendor sourcing, from request to selection
End-to-end RFP workflow — from vendor initiation through evaluation to final selection, in one place.

In most organisations, vendor selection happens across scattered emails, spreadsheets, and disconnected approvals. There's no single source of truth, no audit trail, and "fair comparison" is mostly aspirational. The RFP module was about pulling all of that into one structured digital workflow — without making it feel like another spreadsheet with rounded corners.

Core thought
Balance control, flexibility, and decision clarity — vendor negotiations are rarely linear, so the system had to support multiple rounds while keeping the full historical context intact.
Round-based negotiation flow
Scroll to zoom  ·  Drag to pan  ·  Double-click to reset
Multiple negotiation rounds with vendors added, removed, and re-evaluated — every change preserved as part of the trail.
Key focus areas
Centralise sourcing
Move scattered approvals and emails into one shared workflow.
Round-based negotiations
Multiple rounds, full history preserved — additions, removals, evolving quotes all tracked.
Side-by-side comparison
Structured views to compare pricing, scope, and progress across vendors at once.
Decision confidence
Surface pricing, ratings, and progress clearly so the "why" behind a shortlist is visible.
Audit trail by default
Status changes and vendor movement visible without anyone having to maintain it manually.
Reduce manual effort
Carry forward details from previous stages — less re-entry, fewer errors.
Design challenge

Procurement users process large amounts of vendor data quickly. I leaned on information hierarchy — clear sections, progressive disclosure, summary-led views — so the density felt scannable instead of suffocating.

Shipped
0 → 1 module ~2 months end-to-end First reference for similar workflows
Pattern 03
Contextual Right Drawer
An interaction pattern I proposed and prototyped
The drawer slides over the current screen instead of replacing it.

While working on procurement, I kept seeing the same friction: users would need a document's context mid-task — to validate an approval, sanity-check a vendor, double-check a number — and every time, they had to leave the current screen and dig somewhere else. The workflow broke every time. So I sketched out a smaller pattern in response.

Design principle
Progressive disclosure — show deeper information only when needed, and keep the main workspace clean and task-focused.

The drawer opens as a secondary layer over the current screen, keeping the workflow intact while surfacing the relevant document detail instantly. Click a document pill, drawer slides in, scan what's needed, dismiss, keep going.

States
Details
History
Supporting's
Three states of the drawer — closed (just a pill), opening (slide-in), active (content visible without leaving the screen).
Focus areas
Reduce navigation friction
Stay on the screen you're acting on.
Faster decisions
Context at the point of action, not three pages away.
Traceability
History and status visible at a glance.
Reusable pattern
Designed so other modules could pick it up with minimal adaptation.
Ownership

Where I held the pen

Both designers shared overall product direction; the following were specifically my areas of ownership — from initial scoping through to developer handoff.

🧭
Translating verbal briefs into clear UX flows
Structuring intent before designing — always returning to what the user actually needs to do.
🗺️
Workflows across marketing and finance
Mapping complex, interconnected flows into something different personas could navigate without re-learning the system.
🧩
Contributing to the design system
Building consistency alongside live product work — without ever getting a dedicated "design system phase".
⚡
Supporting faster delivery
Keeping UX aligned with business goals and technical constraints so the team could move without losing clarity.
AI Exploration

AI played two roles

"AI expands possibilities — but clarity comes from constraints."
Working takeaway · Brand Intelle
In the product
  • Explored as a way to improve speed and discoverability across complex modules
  • Required careful thinking about feasibility and user trust
  • Some AI-driven concepts were discarded after feasibility reviews — honestly the right call
  • Even the discarded ideas helped reframe what the product should do
In the design process
  • Used to generate layout directions early in exploration
  • Sped up ideation, freeing time for the harder work of structure
  • AI-generated screens worked as conversation starters with PMs
  • Always treated as a starting point — never a finished direction
What Didn't Work

Grounded by what didn't land

Not everything I worked on landed. These are honest reflections from my own workstreams — moments that pushed me toward better scoping, earlier engineering conversations, and more deliberate ownership.

Early AI concepts were too ambitious
Some ideas explored AI in ways that just weren't buildable at the time. I learned to design for what could actually ship.
A few flows assumed more system capability than existed
Directions had to be walked back after technical conversations. The lesson was simple: bring engineering in earlier.
Iterations grew when scope kept evolving
Learning to scope tighter — and saying "let's name what's in this version" — became one of the most useful skills the project gave me.
Learnings

Systems over screens

Working in a team taught me as much as the work itself. This project reshaped how I think about ownership, collaboration, and when to align versus when to just keep moving.

Before
Thinking in screens.
After
Thinking in systems — every screen evaluated in relation to the ecosystem around it.
Before
Waiting for complete inputs.
After
Comfortable with incomplete inputs — moving forward with imperfect information and structuring well enough to decide.
Before
Prioritising visual polish.
After
Structure first — a well-structured flow with rough visuals beats a polished screen with broken logic.
What's Next

Still figuring things out — and enjoying it

Looking ahead
Environments where things are still being figured out
This project reinforced that I enjoy working in environments where things are still being figured out — small teams, complex problems, design decisions that carry real weight. Below are a few areas I'm actively curious about going deeper in.
Healthcare UX
Clinical & patient workflows
Systems where clarity isn't optional — clinical workflows, patient journeys, and high-stakes decision environments.
AI Workflows
AI-augmented product design
Moving past AI as a feature — products where AI handles complexity in the background while the experience stays simple and trustworthy.
Research-led
Behaviour & systems research
Projects where the design direction is led by what people actually do — not what they say they'll do.
